name: Video walkthrough / product tour
description: Self-service tour for early-stage buyers. Birdie and Sona use this.
conversion_rate_data: Self-service product tours and video walkthroughs are among the most effective B2B SaaS conversion tools. Interactive walkthroughs drive 42% higher demo-to-opportunity conversion compared to static linear video formats (Walnut, 2025). Ungated interactive tours influence 78% of B2B SaaS deals before the first sales contact occurs (Navattic, 2025). Nearly 90% of B2B buyers prefer interactive demos to learn about a product, and 82% want to explore a SaaS product independently before engaging with sales. Companies using video-first product-led growth motions report 35% higher annual recurring revenue growth (Advids, 2026). Hireology found that opportunities involving product tours close 50% faster than those without. Embedding CTA buttons inside video players increases click-through rates by 28%. 72% of B2B buyers now expect a self-serve, digital-first experience when evaluating software. For care sector buyers specifically, no published conversion benchmarks exist, but the principle of self-service exploration applies strongly to a risk-averse audience that wants to understand a product before committing to a sales conversation.
best_for_audience: Best suited for registered managers and operations directors in the early exploration phase — they want to understand what a product or service looks like in practice before committing time to a sales call. Also effective for compliance managers who want to see specific features (e.g., CQC evidence assembly, care plan templates). Less effective for CEO/owners who are more interested in ROI and strategic outcomes than product interface details. The self-service nature respects the time constraints of care professionals — they can explore during a break or after shift without booking a meeting. Particularly valuable for buyers who have been burned by previous software implementations and want to see proof before engaging.
friction_level: Low — the most effective product tours are ungated, allowing immediate access without email registration. Birdie offers 10 ungated interactive product tours accessible via 'Launch tour' buttons with no form required. This zero-friction approach maximises engagement and builds trust. Email capture happens at natural conversion points within or after the tour ('Want to see this with your own data? Book a personalised demo'). Gating the tour itself is counterproductive for care sector buyers who are already wary of sales processes.
industry_examples: Birdie (home care platform) offers a comprehensive product tour experience at birdie.care/product-tours — 10 interactive, ungated tours covering care planning, finance operations, rostering, analytics, medication delivery, client feedback, and staff onboarding. Each tour is self-guided and accessible via a 'Launch tour' button. Birdie also publishes video content at birdie.care/resources/videos and hosts webinars. Sona (workforce management for frontline teams including care homes) offers 'Sona in 60 Seconds' — a short video walkthrough of their platform covering live scheduling, absence management, instant messaging, and shift booking. Sona also offers personalised demos via getsona.com. In B2B SaaS more broadly: Navattic, Walnut, Reprise, Tourial, and Arcade are dedicated platforms for creating interactive product tours. Demogo offers tools to convert video tours into interactive walkthroughs. The trend is clearly moving from passive video toward interactive, clickable product experiences.
care_sector_relevance: High relevance with important implementation considerations. Care sector buyers are non-technical, time-poor, and risk-averse — they want to see how a product works in practice before investing time in a sales process. A video walkthrough or interactive tour that shows real care scenarios (e.g., 'Here's how AI helps you write a care plan review in 10 minutes instead of 2 hours') is far more compelling than feature lists or screenshots. The tour should use care-specific language and scenarios — showing a care home dashboard, resident records, CQC evidence folders — rather than generic SaaS interface demos. However, for an AI agency like Clarica (which builds bespoke solutions rather than selling a single product), the tour format needs adaptation: it could show example outputs, before-and-after workflows, or a guided walkthrough of a typical AI implementation rather than a standard product interface.
ai_trust_factor: Very high. A video walkthrough or product tour is one of the most effective ways to demystify AI for care sector buyers. Seeing AI in action — watching a care plan draft being generated, observing how an AI tool flags compliance gaps, or seeing a before-and-after of admin workload — transforms abstract AI promises into tangible, understandable reality. The visual format bypasses the need for technical literacy. For Clarica specifically, a tour showing 'Here's what AI-assisted care plan writing looks like' with a real interface and real output directly addresses the fear of the unknown. Including human touchpoints in the tour ('the AI drafts, the care manager reviews and approves') reinforces that AI supports rather than replaces staff.
funnel_stage: Awareness to consideration — product tours work best for buyers who have some awareness of the problem (admin burden, compliance workload) and are beginning to explore solutions. The tour moves them from 'I didn't know this was possible' to 'I want to learn more about how this could work for us.' It naturally leads to deeper engagement CTAs: 'Book a personalised demo' or 'Talk to our team about your specific needs.'
mobile_optimization_priority: High priority. Care managers often discover providers on mobile during breaks. Interactive tours must be touch-friendly — large tap targets, swipe navigation, and no hover-dependent interactions. Video walkthroughs should be optimised for mobile viewing: vertical or square format, large text overlays, subtitles (many care workers browse with sound off), and short duration (60–90 seconds for initial hook, with option to explore deeper). Birdie's product tours work on mobile with responsive layouts. Avoid tours that require desktop-style clicking or precise mouse interactions. A 'Watch in 60 seconds' video entry point works well on mobile, with an option to explore a fuller interactive tour on desktop.
